title: Generic Ranking + Realtime WebSocket Push Architecture
topic: Generic drag-and-drop ranking + realtime websocket push as a cross-cutting platform capability — applies to work items, defects, portfolio hierarchy, library items, and any future orderable resource. Includes the 22 stories (00202–00223) decomposed across SQL, API, WebSocket, Frontend, and DEV layers, with action-plan breakdown in dependency order.
summary: Cross-cutting ranking + realtime push platform capability — schema, generic registry, websocket fan-out, 22-story action plan, security review.
content:
Contents
  1. 1. Overview
  2. 2. Key Findings
  3. 3. Detailed Analysis
  4. 4. Architecture Decisions for MMFFDev
  5. 5. Action Plan — 22 Stories in Dependency Order
  6. 6. Security Risks & Mitigations
  7. 7. Sources
  8. 8. Gaps

1. Overview

Drag-and-drop reordering with realtime collaboration is a cross-cutting platform capability that must work identically across every orderable resource — work items, defects, portfolio hierarchy, library items, and any future ranked list. This paper documents the chosen technique (two-column position schema + Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY fan-out + generic resource registry) and lays out a 22-story action plan to ship it in dependency order.

The decision to build a single generic ranking package rather than per-resource implementations is driven by the "no debt" rule: with at least four known consumers (work items, defects, portfolio levels, library items), abstracting upfront is not speculative — it is correct. The first adopter is the work-items tree; subsequent adopters plug in via a 10-line ranking.Register(...) call.

2. Key Findings

  • Gap-based integer positions are simpler than LexoRank for v1 — Atlassian's LexoRank uses base-36 strings to make rebalances vanishingly rare, but at the cost of bucket-based string-comparison gymnastics. For our scale and the "no debt" constraint, two nullable INT columns (backlog_position, sprint_position) with gap = 100 and triggered rebalance are sufficient. Migration to LexoRank is a future option if rebalance frequency becomes a problem.
  • Two-column scope schema preserves historic order without an audit table — when a story is in a sprint, sprint_position is set and backlog_position is NULL; vice versa for backlog. When a sprint closes, sprint_position is NEVER nulled, preserving the historic priority view for free.
  • Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY is sufficient for v1, scalable to thousands of clients per server — A single LISTEN connection multiplexed across all WebSocket clients in one Go process avoids the "one DB connection per listener" antipattern. Redis Pub/Sub is the next step if we exceed one process; we don't need it now.
  • Generic resource_type registry is the right abstraction — Every adopter calls ranking.Register("defect", ResourceConfig{Table:"defects", ScopeColumns:..., PermissionFn:...}) at init. Permission predicates and table mappings live with the adopter, not the ranking package. No copy-paste per resource.
  • Optimistic UI with last-write-wins reconcile is the dominant pattern — Reorder the local list immediately on drop, send the move request, and replace local state with the server's canonical response if it differs. This is what Linear, Notion, and Asana do.
  • Cross-tenant data leak is the highest-priority risk — NOTIFY is global; the bridge MUST filter by org_id before forwarding to subscribers. A canary test that asserts "org A subscriber receives zero messages when org B mutates" is mandatory and must run on every CI build.
  • Subtree bundling is a UI illusion — Dragging a parent visually carries its children, but only the parent's position changes server-side. Children stay attached via parent_id and are not re-ranked. This makes the wire payload tiny (one resource ID) and the server logic trivial.

3. Detailed Analysis

3.1 Position storage: two-column scope vs single global rank

Atlassian's Rally and Jira historically use a single global rank field. The well-documented complaint is that reordering inside a sprint mutates the underlying backlog order — which is "by design" according to Broadcom but creates UX friction and prevents historic order preservation without a separate audit log.

The two-column convention solves this: each row carries up to two position values, and a check constraint enforces exactly-one-non-null based on the active scope column (e.g., sprint_id IS NULL). When a sprint closes, the position is preserved untouched — the historic view is free.

3.2 Generic ranking package shape

The Go package backend/internal/ranking exposes:

// Public API — resource-agnostic
ranking.Register(resourceType string, cfg ResourceConfig)
ranking.MoveToTop(ctx, orgID, resourceType, resourceID, scope)
ranking.MoveAbove(ctx, orgID, resourceType, resourceID, scope, targetID)
ranking.MoveBelow(ctx, orgID, resourceType, resourceID, scope, targetID)
ranking.MoveToBottom(ctx, orgID, resourceType, resourceID, scope)

type ResourceConfig struct {
    Table        string
    OrgIDColumn  string
    ScopeColumns map[string]string  // scopeType -> columnName
    PermissionFn func(ctx, userID, orgID, resourceID) error
}

Adopters register at init. The handler at POST /api/rank/move dispatches by resource_type, calls the registered PermissionFn, then the rank service. Unknown types return error_code: rank.unknown_resource_type.

3.3 LISTEN/NOTIFY fan-out architecture

A single LISTEN rank_changed goroutine in each backend process receives every position-change NOTIFY for every tenant. The bridge filters by org_id (from the payload) against an in-memory subscription map keyed by (org_id, scope_type, scope_id), then forwards to matching WebSocket connections.

Trigger payload schema is generic — works for any resource, not just work items:

{
  "resource_type": "work_item",
  "resource_id": "...",
  "scope_type": "sprint",
  "scope_id": "...",
  "org_id": "..."
}

3.4 Concurrency: SELECT FOR UPDATE on scope key

Two simultaneous "move to top" requests in the same scope are serialised by acquiring a row lock on the scope (sprint row) or an org-keyed advisory lock (for backlog) before computing the new position. The lock key is derived from (org_id, scope_type, scope_id) with org_id sourced from the authenticated session — never the request body — so cross-tenant collisions are impossible.

Last-write-wins: the client's proposed position is advisory; the server computes the final value and returns it. The frontend reconciles if the server's response differs.

3.5 Optimistic UI on the client

The shared useResourceRank({ resourceType, scope }) hook wires HTML5 drag events into the generic POST /api/rank/move. On drop:

  1. Reorder local state immediately (visual feedback).
  2. Send the move request with before or after target IDs.
  3. If the server's response differs (another writer won the race), replace local state with the server's canonical order.

v1 is same-parent reorder only. Reparenting (drop onto a different parent or different depth) is rejected with no visual feedback during hover. This keeps the wire format simple — no cycle prevention needed.

4. Architecture Decisions for MMFFDev

DecisionChoiceRationale
Position storageTwo nullable INT columns + check constraintPreserves historic scope order without an audit table; simpler than LexoRank for v1.
Rank algorithmGap-based integers (gap = 100), rebalance when gap < 2Trivial to implement; rebalance is rare at our scale.
ConcurrencySELECT FOR UPDATE on scope row + advisory lock for backlog; last-write-winsDeterministic final order; no lost writes; lock keys include org_id for tenant isolation.
Realtime channelPostgres LISTEN/NOTIFY, single LISTEN goroutine per processNo new infrastructure; sufficient for thousands of clients per process; Redis is a future option.
Generic abstractionranking.Register(resourceType, ResourceConfig) at init time onlyCompile-time registration prevents runtime registry tampering (a security risk).
Permission modelPer-resource-type PermissionFn called before every DB writeNo default-allow; each adopter declares its own gate.
Frontend abstractionThree shared primitives: <DragHandleColumn />, useResourceRank(), useRealtimeSubscription()Any list/tree gets drag + realtime by importing the trio.
Subtree behaviourVisual-only bundling; only the parent's position changes server-sideTiny payload; trivial server logic; descendants tracked by parent_id.
WebSocket authExisting session cookie + Origin allowlist on upgradeNo new auth surface; CSRF mitigated via Origin check.
Cross-org isolationNOTIFY bridge filters by org_id before forwarding; mandatory canary testHighest-priority security risk; tested on every CI build.

5. Action Plan — 22 Stories in Dependency Order

All stories carry FE-RNK-0001 (cross-cutting ranking) plus their layer label. Each is in Planka Backlog, ready for review. Cards in dependency order:

5.1 Phase 1 — Schema convention (SQL)

IDTitleESTRISKAreas
00202SQL: standard position columns convention for orderable resourcesF3MEDFE-SQL-0010, FE-RNK-0001
00203SQL: standard NOTIFY trigger convention for orderable resourcesF2LOWFE-SQL-0010, FE-RNK-0001

5.2 Phase 2 — Generic ranking package (API)

IDTitleESTRISKAreas
00222Backend: ranking resource registry contract + permission predicate interfaceF3MEDFE-API-0015, FE-RNK-0001
00204Backend: generic rank service (MoveToTop/Above/Below/Bottom) over a resource registryF5MEDFE-API-0015, FE-RNK-0001
00205Backend: rank service uses SELECT FOR UPDATE on scope key for last-write-winsF5HIGHFE-API-0015, FE-RNK-0001
00206Backend: generic POST /api/rank/move endpoint (resource_type + scope + before/after)F5MEDFE-API-0015, FE-RNK-0001
00207Backend: orderable list endpoints order by position columns conventionF3LOWFE-API-0015, FE-RNK-0001
00208Backend: scope-transition rules for adopters (clear inactive scope, append on new scope)F5HIGHFE-API-0015, FE-RNK-0001

5.3 Phase 3 — WebSocket hub (API)

IDTitleESTRISKAreas
00209Backend: WebSocket /ws endpoint with session auth and resource-agnostic subscriptionsF8HIGHFE-API-0016, FE-RNK-0001
00210Backend: WebSocket hub heartbeat + graceful drain on SIGTERMF5MEDFE-API-0016, FE-RNK-0001
00211Backend: LISTEN/NOTIFY bridge fans rank_changed payloads to subscribed connectionsF5MEDFE-API-0016, FE-RNK-0001
00212Backend: every write to an adopter resource emits a rank_changed NOTIFYF3MEDFE-API-0016, FE-RNK-0001

5.4 Phase 4 — Frontend primitives (UI / ITM)

IDTitleESTRISKAreas
00213Frontend: shared <DragHandleColumn /> component usable in any list/treeF2LOWFE-ITM-0006, FE-RNK-0001
00214Frontend: shared useResourceRank() hook for HTML5 drag mechanicsF8MEDFE-ITM-0006, FE-RNK-0001
00215Frontend: subtree-with-parent ghost preview during dragF5MEDFE-ITM-0006, FE-RNK-0001
00216Frontend: optimistic UI on drop with reconcile from server responseF5MEDFE-ITM-0006, FE-RNK-0001
00217Frontend: shared useRealtimeSubscription() hook with reconnect/backoffF5MEDFE-UI-0003, FE-RNK-0001
00218Frontend: refetch-on-push pattern for any subscribed resourceF3LOWFE-UI-0003, FE-RNK-0001

5.5 Phase 5 — Test infrastructure (DEV)

IDTitleESTRISKAreas
00219DEV: backend integration test — two concurrent moves prove last-write-winsF3LOWFE-DEV-0005, FE-RNK-0001
00220DEV: Playwright test for drag-and-drop + subtree bundlingF5MEDFE-DEV-0005, FE-RNK-0001
00221DEV: WebSocket integration test for subscribe / authz / publish / cross-org isolationF3MEDFE-DEV-0005, FE-RNK-0001
00223DEV: docs/c_c_ranking.md — onboarding guide for new orderable resourcesF2LOWFE-DEV-0005, FE-RNK-0001

5.6 Build order summary

  1. Schema first (00202, 00203) — every other layer depends on the position columns and NOTIFY trigger.
  2. Registry contract (00222) — the rank service depends on the ResourceConfig type.
  3. Rank service (00204 → 00205 → 00206 → 00207 → 00208) — internal logic, then HTTP surface, then list ordering, then scope transitions.
  4. WebSocket plumbing (00209 → 00210 → 00211 → 00212) — endpoint + auth, then heartbeat, then bridge, then publish hooks.
  5. Frontend primitives (00213 → 00214 → 00215 → 00216 in parallel with 00217 → 00218) — drag UI and realtime UI are independent.
  6. Tests + docs (00219, 00220, 00221, 00223) — concurrent and Playwright tests after the layer they cover lands; onboarding doc last.

6. Security Risks & Mitigations

Surfaced during security review prior to implementation. Risks 1–4 are HIGH; risks 5–10 are MED–LOW.

#RiskSeverityMitigationCovered by
1Authorization bypass via resource_type spoofingHIGHPer-resource-type PermissionFn called before any DB write; no default-allow.00222, 00206
2Cross-tenant rank corruption via forged org_idHIGHorg_id always derived from session; never request body. Lock keys include org_id.00205, 00206
3WebSocket subscription escalation (subscribe to other tenants' resources)HIGHSubscribe gated by same RBAC as GET /sprints/:id; reject + close on repeated unauthorised subscribes.00209
4Cross-org data leak via NOTIFY fan-outHIGHBridge filters by org_id before forwarding; mandatory CI canary test.00211, 00221
5DoS via subscription floodMEDPer-connection cap (50 active subs); per-IP connection cap.00209
6DoS via rebalance amplificationMEDRate-limit move per user (30/min); base-94 rank migration is a future option.00206
7WebSocket CSRF via cross-site upgradeMEDOrigin allowlist on upgrade; reject any origin not on list.00209
8Audit gap (drag-to-reorder is authority-bearing)MEDAppend-only o_audit_rank_changes row per move (future story; not in v1).(deferred)
9Realtime push as side-channelLOW–MEDAcceptable for v1; flag for re-review when handling regulated data.(documented)
10Generic registry as untrusted-input vectorLOWRegistry is compile-time only; no DB-driven registration.00222

7. Sources

  1. LexoRanks — what are they and how to use them for efficient list sorting (Whisper Arts, Medium)
  2. Lexorank — Managing Sorted Tables With Ease (Çağdaş Alagöz, Turkcell Engineering)
  3. peerreynders/lexg-rank — Exploration of a LexoRank variant (GitHub)
  4. kvandake/lexorank-ts — Reference TypeScript implementation of Jira's LexoRank (GitHub)
  5. PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY: Real-Time Without the Message Broker (Pedro Alonso)
  6. The Notifier Pattern for Applications That Use Postgres (brandur.org)
  7. diogob/postgres-websockets — PostgreSQL + Websockets bridge (GitHub)
  8. Postgres adapter for Socket.IO (official docs)
  9. PostgreSQL: Documentation 18 — NOTIFY (postgresql.org)
  10. Building an Optimistic UI with RxDB (RxDB)
  11. Optimistic updates with concurrency control (Alan Torres, First Resonance Engineering)
  12. What You Need to Know About Optimistic Concurrency (DEV Community)
  13. Drag & Drop UX Design Best Practices (Pencil & Paper)
  14. Drag-and-drop pattern (Cloudscape Design System, AWS)
  15. Drag and drop guidelines (PatternFly)

8. Gaps

  • Quantitative scaling threshold for single-process LISTEN multiplex — Sources confirm the pattern is "sufficient for thousands of concurrent listeners with one shared connection" but no source gave a hard numeric ceiling. We will measure during load testing rather than guess.
  • Audit-trail story (risk #8) — Deferred to a follow-up; not currently in the 22-story plan. Should be raised before the feature is enabled for regulated tenants.
  • Mobile drag UX — HTML5 drag-and-drop API has known mobile-Safari quirks. v1 plan is desktop-first; mobile is a separate follow-up using touch events.
  • Reparenting (changing parent during drag) — Explicitly out of scope for v1 to avoid cycle-prevention complexity. A future story will add it.
  • Comparison against Linear's specific implementation — Linear is closed-source; we triangulated from blog posts and inferred behaviour, not source. Treat "Linear does X" claims as best-guess.
